_meta.title: Experimentation Framework
_meta.author: Caio, Claude (collaborative)
_meta.danilo_assessment: Template only — experiment card + empty log. No methodology, no decision rules, no learning system.

cross_references.m10_price_testing: data/m10_pricing_offer/price_testing_framework.md — price tests use M10 protocol, NOT this framework
cross_references.m12_creative_testing: ops_model_v10_dump/348_creative_testing_protocol1.json — creative testing specifics live in M14 creative_testing_protocol.md

core_philosophy.tagline: Every experiment produces learning, not just winners.

principles.name: Learning over winning
principles.description: A test that confirms 'no difference' is not a failure — it eliminates a hypothesis and frees resources. The only failed test is one with no learning documented.
principles.anti_pattern: Declaring tests 'inconclusive' and moving on without documenting what was learned.

principles.name: Big swings before small tweaks
principles.description: Test fundamentally different approaches (different angles, different formats, different audiences) before optimizing details (button color, font size). At low traffic, you can't detect small effects — so only test things that COULD produce large effects.
principles.anti_pattern: Testing button colors when you haven't validated your headline or offer.

principles.name: Sequential price, parallel creative
principles.description: Price tests must run one at a time (they contaminate each other). Creative tests and presentation tests can run in parallel because they don't interact. See M10 Price Testing Framework for price-specific protocol.
principles.anti_pattern: Running two price tests simultaneously, or blocking creative tests behind a price test queue.

principles.name: Document everything, even failures
principles.description: Every test gets a card filed in the test log before launch. Every test gets results + learning documented after completion. This builds institutional knowledge that compounds.
principles.anti_pattern: Running tests ad-hoc in the ad platform and never recording results.

principles.name: Match method to traffic
principles.description: At <100 visitors/day, traditional A/B testing cannot detect modest effects. Use qualitative judgment, pre/post testing, or Bayesian methods. Don't pretend you have statistical power you don't have.
principles.anti_pattern: Waiting 3 months for 'statistical significance' on a landing page test when you could have shipped the obvious winner in week 1.

principles.name: Test the hypothesis, not the tactic
principles.description: Frame every test as a hypothesis about customer behavior, not a preference between creative executions. 'Users trust UGC more than founder content' is testable. 'This ad looks cool' is not.
principles.anti_pattern: Testing random variations without a hypothesis about WHY the variant should win.

experiment_card.description: Every experiment must have a card filed BEFORE launch. No exceptions.

schema.hypothesis: If we [change X], then [metric Y] will [increase/decrease] because [reason Z]
schema.test_type: One of: price | creative | landing_page | audience | email | checkout | general

variants.additional_variants: Optional (C, D — only if traffic supports it)

schema.primary_metric: The ONE metric that decides the winner
schema.secondary_metrics: Diagnostic metrics that help explain WHY
schema.sample_size_target: Minimum visitors/impressions per variant before decision
schema.kill_criteria: When to stop early (optional — for sequential analysis)
schema.decision_date: When we commit to reading results
schema.owner: Who monitors this test and makes the call

example.hypothesis: If we use a 'tired by 2pm' hook instead of '78 minerals' hook, CTR will increase by >30% because fatigue is a more visceral problem than mineral count

variants.control: '78 minerals your body is missing' hook — UGC format
variants.challenger: 'Tired by 2pm? Your electrolytes are wrong.' hook — same UGC format

example.kill_criteria: Kill variant if CTR <0.5% after 1,000 impressions

decision_framework.description: How to decide what to test, and how to interpret results at different traffic levels.

testing_priority_matrix.description: What to test first. Ordered by expected impact × ease of testing.

priority_tiers.name: Test FIRST — Highest impact, fastest learning

items.why_first: Hook determines if anyone sees your ad. 70-80% of performance is in the first 3 seconds. Testable with $50-100 per variant.
$10 price change = ~17% revenue swing per order. Use M10 protocol (sequential, RPV as primary metric).
First text visitors read. Can produce 20-50% conversion lift from one change.

priority_tiers.name: Test SECOND — High impact, moderate effort

items.why: Same format, different persuasion approach (health benefit vs social proof vs fear of missing out).
UGC vs founder-to-camera vs lifestyle B-roll vs static image. Format determines ad production investment.
Product shot vs lifestyle vs ingredient close-up. Visual hierarchy drives attention.
Which is the pre-selected option? Can dramatically shift subscription rate (HYP-002).

priority_tiers.name: Test LATER — Medium impact, or requires more traffic
Do NOT test in first 6 months

items.why_not: Effect size too small to detect at low traffic. Any 'winner' is noise.
No meaningful conversion impact at any traffic level.
Difference between 'Buy Now' and 'Add to Cart' is <1% — undetectable without 50K+ visitors.
If you change 10 things, you learn nothing about what worked.

traffic_based_methodology.description: Match your testing methodology to your actual traffic. Don't pretend to have statistical power you don't have.

tiers.approach: No A/B testing. Ship your best guess and iterate based on qualitative signals (heatmaps, session recordings, customer feedback, sales conversations).
tiers.when_to_use: Pre-launch through Month 1. Also for any change where the 'right answer' is obvious (fixing a broken flow, adding missing information).
tiers.key_rule: If a change is obviously better (e.g., fixing a 404, adding a missing CTA), JUST SHIP IT. Don't 'test' obvious improvements.

tiers.approach: For landing page changes: implement the change and compare 2-week windows (before vs after). For ad creative: use platform-native testing (Meta A/B, TikTok split test) which has enough volume from ad delivery. For critical decisions: use Bayesian analysis with informative priors.

sample_size_guidance.ad_creative_test: 1,000+ impressions per variant (achievable in 2-3 days at $50/day)
sample_size_guidance.note: At these volumes, you can detect LARGE effects (>30% relative change) but NOT moderate effects (<15%).

tiers.key_rule: Only test big swings. If you think a change might improve things by 5%, don't bother testing — just ship it.

tiers.approach: Traditional A/B testing becomes viable for high-impact elements. Use Bayesian for faster decisions on creative tests. Sequential analysis for price tests (M10 protocol).

sample_size_guidance.price_test: 2,000+ visitors per variant (use M10 protocol, 14-30 days)

tiers.key_rule: You can now detect ~15-20% relative effects. Run 2-4 tests per month across ad creative + landing page.

tiers.approach: Multiple concurrent tests across ad creative, landing page, email, and checkout. Price tests still sequential (M10). Creative tests scaled to 5-10 new variants per week.
tiers.key_rule: Testing velocity is now your competitive advantage. Aim for 8-12 tests per month.

frequentist.when_to_use: Price tests, checkout changes — anywhere a wrong decision has high financial impact.
frequentist.peeking_policy: No peeking before planned sample size. Use sequential analysis if early stopping needed.

sample_size_table.description: Required visitors PER VARIANT at 95% confidence, 80% power
sample_size_table.note: These are per-variant numbers. Double for total test traffic.

bayesian.prior: Weakly informative — Beta(1,1) for new tests, Beta(alpha, beta) from previous test data for iterations
bayesian.when_to_use: Creative testing, email subject lines, audience tests — anywhere faster decisions are worth the trade-off of slightly less certainty.
advantages_at_low_traffic: Can declare results sooner (no fixed sample size)
Intuitive probability output ('93% chance variant B is better')
Handles early stopping without inflating false positive rate
Can incorporate prior knowledge from previous tests
bayesian.practical_note: At 500 impressions per variant, Bayesian can often give you 80%+ confidence on a 30%+ effect. Frequentist would say 'not enough data.' For creative testing where the cost of a wrong decision is low (just run another creative), 80% is good enough.

error_rate_disclosure.note: DIALOGUE UPGRADE UPG-1: Be explicit about what each confidence threshold COSTS you in false decisions.

95%.use_when: Pricing, checkout, subscription terms — anywhere a wrong decision costs revenue. MANDATORY for all M10 price tests.

90%.use_when: Landing page changes, offer framing — moderate stakes, moderately reversible.

80%.use_when: Ad creative selection — low stakes, easily reversible. At 5 creatives/month, expect ~1 false winner per quarter. Acceptable because: (a) creative swaps are instant, (b) the 'wrong' creative still ran at near-breakeven, (c) you'll detect the error within days of scaling.

75%.use_when: ONLY for creative screening (kill/continue decisions on Day 3). NOT for final ship/no-ship decisions.

the_just_ship_it_threshold.description: Not everything needs a test. Use judgment when:
criteria: The change is obviously better (fixing broken UX, adding missing info)
The potential downside of being wrong is tiny (email copy tweak)
You'd need 6+ months of traffic to detect the effect size
You're deciding between two options and neither is risky
Customer feedback overwhelmingly points in one direction
the_just_ship_it_threshold.warning: Never 'just ship it' on: price changes, subscription terms, checkout flow changes, or anything that affects revenue per visitor. These always get tested.

testing_velocity_benchmarks.description: How many tests per month by growth stage

benchmarks.focus: Ad creative testing only (platform has the traffic). Ship best-guess site.
Find winning creative angles. Test 1 landing page element per month (big changes only).
Testing program becomes systematic. Run creative and landing page tests in parallel.
Full experimentation program. Iterating on email, checkout, upsell in addition to creative + LP.

learning_system.description: How we capture and compound knowledge from every test

components.name: Test Log
components.purpose: Chronological record of every experiment with results and learnings

components.name: Knowledge Library
components.purpose: Accumulated learnings organized by domain (creative, landing page, email, audience)
components.structure: Each learning has: domain, finding, confidence, source_test_id, date, implications

components.name: Quarterly Review
components.purpose: Every 3 months, review test log. Identify: (1) what domains produced most wins, (2) what testing assumptions were wrong, (3) what to test next quarter.

naming_conventions.test_ids: {TYPE}-{NNN} where TYPE is PT/CT/LP/AT/EM/CK/GN and NNN is sequential
naming_conventions.creative_ids: C{NNN} — every creative variant gets a unique ID for lifetime tracking
naming_conventions.note: Naming convention enables filtering, searching, and pattern recognition across hundreds of tests over time.

anti_patterns.description: Common experimentation mistakes that destroy learning

mistakes.name: The HiPPO
mistakes.description: Highest Paid Person's Opinion overrides test data. 'I don't like blue buttons' trumps data showing blue converts better.
mistakes.fix: Pre-register the decision rule before the test starts. Document: 'If variant B wins by X%, we implement it regardless of aesthetic preference.'

mistakes.name: Peeking and stopping
mistakes.description: Checking results daily and stopping the test as soon as one variant is 'ahead.' This inflates false positives to 20-30%.
mistakes.fix: Set sample size and duration BEFORE launch. Use sequential analysis methods if early stopping is needed.

mistakes.name: Testing too many things at once
mistakes.description: Changing headline + image + CTA + layout simultaneously. Even if the new version wins, you don't know WHY.
mistakes.fix: One variable at a time for learning. Only test multiple changes when you need speed over insight (and accept reduced learning).

mistakes.name: The button color trap
mistakes.description: Spending limited testing bandwidth on low-impact elements. Button color differences are <1% — undetectable at IonWave's traffic for months.
mistakes.fix: Priority matrix (Tier 1 first). If the expected effect is <10%, don't test it until you have 500+ daily visitors.

mistakes.name: Survivorship bias in creative
mistakes.description: Only studying winning creatives. The losers teach you equally — they show what DOESN'T resonate.
mistakes.fix: Document learnings from losers in the test log. Pattern-match across failures to find anti-patterns.

mistakes.name: No documentation
mistakes.description: Running tests in ad platforms without recording hypotheses, results, or learnings. Institutional knowledge = zero.
mistakes.fix: Experiment card filed BEFORE launch. Results + learning filed WITHIN 48 hours of test completion.

intelligence_gaps.description: No real test data yet — all frameworks are theoretical
intelligence_gaps.upgrade_path: Run first 5 creative tests → calibrate kill criteria, sample sizes, and velocity benchmarks with real IonWave data

intelligence_gaps.description: Bayesian priors not calibrated for marine plasma supplement category
intelligence_gaps.upgrade_path: After 10+ tests, build category-specific priors from IonWave's own data. Until then, use weakly informative priors.

intelligence_gaps.description: Creative fatigue rates unknown for this category
intelligence_gaps.upgrade_path: Track winning creative lifespan. After 5 winners decay, we'll have our own fatigue curve.
